catalog.title: Culture & context · Ask better questions about NL life
catalog.description: Ask better questions about NL life · A2 culture & context in “Admin & services”: Dutch you can use the same week, guided steps, and a short check at the end.

pedagogy.objective: By the end of this lesson, you can connect language about admin & services to situated Netherlands context and apply it in a short task
can_do_outcomes: Ask what documents you need for a simple gemeente appointment
Request a duplicate card or change of address in simple phrases
Link cultural context to memorisable Dutch phrases.

grammar_points.point: conditional polite forms (Ik zou graag … willen)
examples_nl: Ik zou graag een afspraak willen voor een ID-kaart.
Zou u dit formulier kunnen controleren?
examples_en: I'd like an appointment for an ID card.

examples_nl: Het document dat u nodig heeft, staat online.
De pasfoto die ik meeneem, is recent.
examples_en: The document you need is online.
The passport photo I'm bringing is recent.

examples_nl: Ik moet mijn adres bij de gemeente wijzigen.
Zonder DigiD loop ik naar het loket.
examples_en: I have to change my address at the municipality.
Without DigiD I go to the desk.

micro_outcomes: I can recognise key words about admin & services in short Dutch texts or audio.
I can copy at least one useful chunk into a sentence about myself.
I can finish most guided tasks in the lesson without peeking at translations on the second try.
I can pass the quick self-check at the end of the lesson.
I know what to revise tomorrow in two minutes or less.

steps.activity:
Welcome! This lesson is built in small steps so you always know where you are.

**What you'll be able to do:** Connect language about admin & services to situated Netherlands context and apply it in a short task

**Grammar thread today:** Polite requests with zou … willen/kunnen — you'll get explanations, examples, and production tasks in **Language focus** and **Guided practice**.

Take your time — you can repeat any step. Try to say at least one Dutch sentence out loud today.
steps.teacher_notes: Keep under ~1 min; reassure anxious learners.

steps.activity:
Let's wake up vocabulary about **Admin & services**.

Words you might use today:
• **wachtrij**
• **bankpas**
• **rekening**
• **overschrijving**
• **postpakket**
• **handtekening**
• **gemeente**
• **paspoort**

**Your turn (1–2 short sentences, Dutch or mixed):**
**Wat deed je gisteren?** — *What did you do yesterday?*

If you only know a few words, that's fine — start there.
steps.example_response: Gisteren ging ik naar de **gemeente** voor een afspraak. Daarna deed ik een kopie van een document en postte ik een brief.

steps.activity:
**Culture snapshot — the Netherlands**

Many gemeenten use DigiD for online services; without DigiD you often need to visit in person with ID and proof of address.

**Mini text (Dutch):**
In veel appartementen staat een huisregel: na 22:00 rust op de gang. Buren lossen kleine problemen vaak eerst met een kort gesprek.

*In many flats there is a rule: quiet in the corridor after 22:00. Neighbours often solve small issues with a short chat first.*

**Pronunciation (listen + repeat)**
**Pronunciation — final -n:** *eten, gaan* — don’t swallow the *-n* completely in careful speech.

**Useful phrases for talking about customs**

• **In Nederland …** — In the Netherlands …
• **Bij ons …** — Where I'm from …
• **Mag ik vragen …?** — May I ask …?

**Context:** Many gemeenten use DigiD for online services; without DigiD you often need to visit in person with ID and proof of address.

**Say or write:** one respectful question you could ask a Dutch friend about a tradition.

**1 — Match:** Koningsdag — orange / Sinterklaas — December gifts / kerst — family meal (discuss).

**2 — Institution:** where do you register an address? *(hint: **gemeente**)*

**3 — Sort:** "Direct can still be polite" — **waar / niet waar**?

**4 — Gist:** what is **rust** on a building notice about?

**5 — Reflect:** one difference between your country and NL (English ok).

**6 — Gap-fill:** In Nederland fietsen veel mensen, _____ het is plat.

**7 — Scenario:** friend cancels last minute — what do many Dutch people appreciate?

**8 — Your question in Dutch:** **Mag ik vragen hoe …?** finish it.

**Culture reminder:** Many gemeenten use DigiD for online services; without DigiD you often need to visit in person with ID and proof of address.
---

**All four skills — finish this lesson with these four micro-tasks**

🎧 **Listening:** Use TTS on this Dutch line (slow, then closer to natural): *Ik zou graag een afspraak willen voor een ID-kaart.*

📖 **Reading:** Read silently, then say the **main idea in one English sentence**: *Ik zou graag een afspraak willen voor een ID-kaart.*

✍️ **Writing:** Write **one new Dutch sentence** that uses **wachtrij** and fits *Polite requests with zou … willen/kunnen*.

🗣️ **Speaking:** Read your new sentence **aloud twice** — exaggerate stress the first time, normal the second.

*(If this lesson was already heavy on one skill, treat that skill as “extra reps” — the point is you always touch all four.)*
steps.teacher_notes: Mix modalities; at least 6 tasks — all concrete above.

steps.activity:
**Phrase bank (use freely)**
• wachtrij
• bankpas
• rekening
• overschrijving
• postpakket
• handtekening
• gemeente
• paspoort
• verblijfsvergunning
• DigiD

**Your task:** Write or say **4–6 short sentences** in Dutch about this unit theme.
Rules: include **at least one question** and one connector (**en**, **maar**, or **want**).

**Example starter (you can change it):**
Vandaag … Ik wil … **want** … **En jij?**

**Quick self-check**

Try each question, then tap **Check answer**. The correct answer stays hidden until you submit.

**Tip:** Say Dutch options out loud if that helps.

**Afterwards:** If three or more felt easy, open **Quiz** for a short recap. If not, go back to **Guided practice** once more.
steps.teacher_notes: Rendered with interactive UI when interaction is present.

items.prompt: Pick the best reply when someone says **Hoe gaat het?**
items.feedbackCorrect: Nice — this is a natural, friendly reply.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Tot ziens** is goodbye; **Graag gedaan** fits after *dank je*. After *Hoe gaat het?* people often say how they feel, e.g. **Goed, dank je!**

items.hint: Present tense of *leren* (to learn), 1st person singular.

items.prompt: Which opening sounds **more formal** when you talk to a stranger or in a shop?
items.feedbackCorrect: Right — **u** + verb form (*Kunt u…*) is the usual polite pattern.
items.feedbackIncorrect: **Kunt u** with *u* is more formal; **Kun je** is fine with friends.

items.prompt: In Dutch, yes/no questions often put the **verb before the subject** (e.g. *Ga je mee?*).
items.feedbackCorrect: Correct — verb-first order is typical for yes/no questions.
items.feedbackIncorrect: This is **true**: many yes/no questions start with the verb.

items.prompt: Say or write **one new sentence** using **wachtrij**.
items.exampleAnswer: Example: *Ik wil graag wachtrij met mijn nieuwe buren.* (adjust to your situation.)

steps.activity:
**Nice work.**

Next in this unit: **Listen & read · Ears and eyes on realistic turns** — we keep building **Admin & services** with a new focus.

**2-minute revision:** skim the phrase bank from step 6 once more.

When you're ready, open **Quiz** for a compact recap — or **Flashcards** for the lemmas.

**Dutch culture insight**

**Paspoort**, **ID**, **BSN**, and **verblijfsvergunning** are vocabulary you hear in real queues. Photocopies (*kopie*) are sometimes needed even when you have scans.

**Phrases — gemeente desk**

| Dutch | English |
|-------|--------|
| *Ik zou graag een afspraak willen.* | I'd like an appointment. |
| *Welke documenten heb ik nodig?* | Which documents do I need? |
| *Kan ik dit online met DigiD?* | Can I do this online with DigiD? |

**Mini dialogue**

**Balie:** Goedemorgen, waarmee kan ik u helpen?
**Inwoner:** Ik wil me inschrijven op dit adres. Wat moet ik laten zien?
**Balie:** Paspoort, huurcontract en een bankafschrift van de afgelopen maand.

**Say or write:** Ask what you need to bring for one appointment.

*Optional reflection:* In one English sentence, what feels similar or different where you live?
steps.teacher_notes: Link to next lesson id in CMS when wired.

quiz_ideas: Self-check: choose the best reply to "Hoe gaat het?" (see step 7 in the lesson).
Gap-fill idea: write a sentence with **wachtrij** in the present tense.
True/false: in Dutch yes/no questions the verb often comes before the subject.
Say one new polite request starting with **Kunt u …** or **Mag ik …**.
Paraphrase one Dutch line from the lesson input in your own English.
assessment.success_criteria: You can complete the step-7 self-check comfortably and produce at least one clear Dutch sentence about the lesson theme (spoken or written).
